authoritative_sources.title: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST CSF 2.0 is official U.S. government cross-industry cybersecurity risk-management guidance and provides the core Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover outcome vocabulary used to frame cloud posture work.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: governance intake, outcome mapping, risk context, and cross-industry terminology
practical_use_in_pack: quality review language that avoids tool-only recommendations

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2: Risk Management Framework for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The RMF is an official process for categorization, control selection, implementation, assessment, authorization, and monitoring, making it authoritative for evidence assembly, monitoring, hold/release caveats, and authorization boundaries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: control evidence intake, monitoring, authorization-boundary handling, and hold or release routing

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 is a recognized public catalog of security and privacy controls used to structure control evidence, residual-risk, compensating control, and control-gap discussions.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: control mapping, evidence sufficiency, residual-risk rationale, and exception planning
practical_use_in_pack: control gap and compensating control outputs

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-144: Guidelines on Security and Privacy in Public Cloud Computing
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-144 directly addresses public-cloud security and privacy considerations, including planning, service models, risk, shared responsibility, data protection, availability, compliance, and portability.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: public-cloud intake, shared responsibility, cloud risk analysis, and tenant evidence planning

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-210: General Access Control Guidance for Cloud Systems
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-210 is official cloud access-control guidance covering cloud access-control characteristics, models, federation, administrative considerations, and policy needs.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: IAM, federation, service identity, privileged access, and effective-permission evidence review

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-207A: A Zero Trust Architecture Model for Access Control in Cloud-Native Applications in Multi-Cloud Environments
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-207A directly addresses access control for cloud-native applications in multi-cloud environments, including API gateways, service mesh, sidecar proxies, identities, and granular policy enforcement.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: cloud-native, multi-cloud, tenant-boundary, and access-control design review
practical_use_in_pack: service identity and API access caveats

authoritative_sources.title: CISA Cross-Sector Cybersecurity Performance Goals
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA CPGs are official cross-sector cybersecurity goals designed to provide prioritized, measurable practices and are appropriate for practical cloud safeguard prioritization across industries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: minimum safeguard review, resilience expectations, and remediation prioritization

authoritative_sources.title: CISA Secure Cloud Business Applications (SCuBA) Project
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA SCuBA is an official cloud security project focused on secure cloud business applications and supplies practical baseline, configuration, visibility, and tenant evidence concepts for cloud business application security.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: tenant baseline review, cloud business application configuration, logging, visibility, and implementation guidance
practical_use_in_pack: SaaS and cloud business application evidence expectations

authoritative_sources.title: CIS Critical Security Controls Version 8.1
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CIS Controls v8.1 is a recognized prioritized safeguard set for practical cyber defense and supports measurable remediation, validation, and implementation-group-aware sequencing.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: control coverage, measurable safeguard validation, and corrective-action prioritization

authoritative_sources.title: CIS Benchmarks for AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform, and Kubernetes
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CIS cloud benchmarks are consensus secure-configuration benchmarks for major cloud platforms and Kubernetes, giving provider-specific evidence expectations and a practical secure-configuration vocabulary.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: cloud provider configuration review, benchmark evidence mapping, and platform-specific caveat handling

authoritative_sources.title: Cloud Security Alliance Cloud Controls Matrix
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CSA CCM is a cloud-specific control framework used to organize cloud security and assurance evidence across cloud service relationships, making it relevant to shared-responsibility and cloud control mapping.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: cloud assurance evidence planning, control mapping, shared responsibility, and tenant-specific cloud control coverage

authoritative_sources.title: AWS Well-Architected Framework: Security Pillar
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AWS official documentation is authoritative for AWS-specific security expectations around shared responsibility, identity, detection, infrastructure protection, data protection, and incident preparation.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: vendor-specific cloud guidance, implementation sequencing, and provider-specific assumption handling

authoritative_sources.title: FedRAMP Rev. 5 Baselines and Templates
authoritative_sources.publisher: Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FedRAMP baselines and templates are authoritative U.S. federal cloud authorization references and are useful for evidence artifact expectations when tenants require federal cloud assurance.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: regulated cloud evidence packaging, POA&M and continuous-monitoring caveats, and no-authorization boundary
practical_use_in_pack: SSP, SAP, SAR, POA&M, and continuous-monitoring evidence awareness

authoritative_sources.title: Current cloud-security-specialist package baseline
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The committed package is authoritative only for legacy supported task, input, output, validation, refusal, and readiness context that issue #1601 required preserving; it is not treated as a public domain authority.

workflow_stages.name: scope, authority, and tenant context intake
workflow_stages.description: Confirm cloud scope, provider, account or subscription boundary, tenant model, data classification, service criticality, request intent, output authority, source precedence, restricted-data constraints, and adjacent-lane indicators before assessment.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: cloud evidence and system-of-record assembly
workflow_stages.description: Collect architecture, data flow, cloud inventory, IAM, network, storage, key, logging, detection, backup, workload, IaC, CSPM, vulnerability, risk, exception, control, and change-gate records from tenant-approved systems.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: control inspection and evidence sufficiency review
workflow_stages.description: Inspect cloud posture against public and tenant-approved baselines, identify satisfied, failed, unclear, blocked, or non-equivalent controls, and record confidence, freshness, assumptions, and evidence gaps.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: risk, prioritization, residual-risk, and exception analysis
workflow_stages.description: Translate posture gaps into prioritized findings using exposure, privilege, exploitability, data sensitivity, business impact, dependency, remediation effort, rollback feasibility, and tenant obligations.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: implementation guidance, deployment safety, and corrective action
workflow_stages.description: Prepare implementation tasks, deployment sequencing, validation signals, monitoring hooks, rollback conditions, quality review notes, control or incident summaries, and corrective-action escalations.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: hold, release, refusal, and orchestrator routing
workflow_stages.description: Recommend hold or escalation when evidence, authority, policy precedence, tenant facts, or safety controls are insufficient; refuse execution or attestation work; and route adjacent-lane requests with evidence context.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: cloud architecture diagrams, data-flow diagrams, landing-zone designs, account or subscription inventory, resource inventory, and service criticality records
IAM policy exports, effective-permission reports, privileged access evidence, service identity records, federation configuration, break-glass access records, and session logs
network topology, security groups, firewall rules, routing, ingress and egress rules, WAF, DNS, private connectivity, segmentation, and exposure inventory
storage, database, object store, public-access, encryption, key management, secrets, certificate, backup, retention, and recovery evidence
cloud audit logs, SIEM summaries, CSPM and CNAPP findings, CIEM reports, vulnerability summaries, alert trends, detection coverage, and recurring defect records
control evidence, benchmark reports, tenant policy, risk register, exception register, POA&M, change-control records, release gates, validation evidence, and approval matrix

prioritized findings, control mapping matrix, residual-risk statement, exception rationale, quality review note, control or incident summary, corrective-action escalation, and rollback-safe hardening playbook
systems_of_record: cloud provider organization, account, subscription, project, resource inventory, and native security center
CSPM, CNAPP, CIEM, vulnerability, exposure management, benchmark, and posture reporting platforms
identity provider, cloud IAM, PAM, service identity registry, and access policy repositories
infrastructure-as-code, configuration, policy-as-code, landing-zone, Kubernetes, and deployment repositories
network source of truth, firewall manager, WAF, DNS, private connectivity, routing, and segmentation policy systems
KMS, secrets management, certificate management, storage, database, backup, retention, and disaster-recovery systems
SIEM, SOAR, cloud audit logging, telemetry, detection engineering, incident tracker, and alert repository
GRC platform, control evidence repository, risk register, exception register, POA&M, audit request tracker, and policy repository
architecture repository, threat model repository, service catalog, CMDB, change management, and release gate system
privacy inventory, data classification system, contractual-obligation inventory, customer commitments, and tenant-approved evidence store

allowed: classify cloud security requests as in-scope, out-of-scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites
assess cloud posture evidence across identity, storage, network, workload, key management, logging, monitoring, backup, resilience, and tenant isolation

prepare evidence-backed prioritized findings, control mappings, implementation tasks, residual-risk rationale, exception rationale, quality review notes, control summaries, corrective-action escalations, and rollback-safe hardening playbooks
recommend hold, release readiness, validation, monitoring, and rollback conditions while preserving final approval authority with named owners
route low-confidence, source-policy conflict, restricted-evidence, regulated-data, or adjacent-specialist work to orchestrator or human reviewer
escalate: tenant facts, cloud evidence, telemetry, source records, or authority context are missing, stale, inaccessible, restricted, or contradictory
public guidance, provider guidance, tenant policy, customer contract, jurisdiction, regulated-data rule, or safety control materially conflicts and no precedence rule is available
hold, release, exception, finding closure, risk acceptance, materiality, compliance, legal, audit, or production approval is requested
cloud provider-specific evidence or benchmark guidance cannot be safely generalized across providers
tenant industry context materially changes evidence sufficiency, privacy, safety, resilience, or contractual obligations
refuse: execute live penetration testing, exploit validation, attack simulation, malware work, social engineering, or adversary tooling
perform incident response containment, eradication, recovery, forensics, breach notification, or production command execution
issue audit opinions, compliance certifications, legal interpretations, regulatory determinations, formal attestation, or authorization statements
own broad non-security cloud architecture, cost optimization, capacity engineering, procurement, platform implementation, production change approval, or executive risk acceptance
bypass hold, release, quality, safety, privacy, or change-control gates without named authority and validation evidence
request, retain, or share restricted tenant evidence without approved access, minimization, retention, redaction, and audit logging controls

domain_failure_modes: generic cloud advice ignores account, subscription, project, tenant model, service criticality, data classification, owner, and source-of-record boundaries
cloud control mapping is overstated as proof of control effectiveness, audit attestation, FedRAMP authorization, legal compliance, or release approval
provider-specific guidance is applied to a different cloud provider or Kubernetes environment without non-equivalence caveats
CSPM or benchmark findings are accepted as complete while IAM effective permissions, logging coverage, data sensitivity, and business impact evidence are missing
public exposure, privileged access, or missing logging is deprioritized because remediation effort is high rather than because risk evidence supports the choice
recurring misconfiguration is treated as a one-off ticket instead of a baseline, policy-as-code, ownership, release-gate, or monitoring failure
hold, finding closure, exception, or release is recommended without validation evidence and named owner authority
active incident response or penetration testing work is absorbed into the advisory lane instead of routed to the right specialist
restricted logs, customer data, architecture records, or secrets are requested or retained without tenant-approved evidence handling controls
cross-industry is treated as generic and tenant-specific regulated-data, privacy, safety, contractual, public-sector, or critical-service caveats are omitted
conflicting public source, vendor guidance, benchmark, tenant policy, customer contract, or source record is synthesized away instead of escalated
multi-tenant guidance permits cross-tenant assumptions, evidence sharing, or weak isolation validation

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: normal in-scope analysis
edge-case analysis with partial evidence
prioritization and recommendation quality
out-of-scope rejection
low-confidence escalation
clear evidence-backed rationale
no unreviewed regressions
Scenario proving output fidelity for: Prioritized findings with control mappings and implementation tasks
Scenario proving output fidelity for: Residual-risk and exception rationale with evidence references
Scenario proving output fidelity for: Escalation decisions for low-confidence, ambiguous, or out-of-scope work
Scenario proving output fidelity for: Deployment and rollback-safe hardening playbooks
Boundary case proving refusal: Do not execute or run live penetration tests
Boundary case proving refusal: Do not execute incident response containment actions in production
Boundary case proving refusal: Do not issue formal audit/compliance attestation statements
Boundary case proving refusal: Do not design broad non-security cloud architecture outside this specialist lane
Escalation case proving orchestrator return: Return to orchestrator when confidence is below threshold
Escalation case proving orchestrator return: Return to orchestrator when request crosses specialty boundary
Insufficient evidence to clear a hold or close a finding
Repeat defect or recurring incident
Request to bypass a documented safety or quality control
Scenario proving industry-specific in-scope execution
Scenario proving escalation when industry-specific authority or safety facts are missing
Scenario proving the lane refuses or escalates when: do not treat the industry name as cosmetic; it must materially change the pack.
Normal in-scope execution using realistic industry terminology and artifact shapes
Boundary rejection when the request should move to an adjacent specialist
Low-confidence handling when required facts, records, or authority sources are missing
Conflicting-source or conflicting-policy handling that requires escalation instead of confident synthesis
Tenant-specific adaptation handling that preserves the public baseline and records assumptions
Industry-specific prerequisite or evidence gap
Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane

unresolved_ambiguities.notes: Cloud security requirements vary by tenant industry, jurisdiction, public-sector status, data type, and customer contract. The specialist can identify caveats and route decisions but cannot issue legal, regulatory, audit, or formal compliance determinations.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: tenant policy precedence over public baselines
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: Tenant policy, risk appetite, exception rules, hold/release gates, and approval matrices may be stricter or different than public baselines. Runtime must retrieve precedence rules or escalate conflicts.

AWS, Azure, GCP, Kubernetes, SaaS, hybrid, and multi-cloud controls are not always equivalent. The specialist must record non-equivalence instead of collapsing platform-specific evidence into a generic mapping.
